title: SEGMENT 16 — Drug discovery: toxicity, target ID, and the path to market

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Drug discovery: toxicity, target ID, and the path to market

Let's talk about drug discovery, because this is where organoids are making, arguably, their biggest practical impact today — not in personalized medicine, not yet, and not in transplantation, also not yet, but in the unglamorous but enormously consequential work of getting better drugs through development.
The drug discovery pipeline is famously brutal.
Of every 10,000 candidate compounds that enter preclinical evaluation, perhaps one reaches the market.

The attrition rates at each stage are enormous, and most of that attrition happens because compounds fail to work in humans, or because they have unacceptable side effects in humans, that weren't predicted by the models used in preclinical development.
The two most common reasons for clinical failure are lack of efficacy and unexpected toxicity.
Both of these are, in principle, addressable by better preclinical models.
Better models predict better; fewer drugs fail late; pipeline economics improve.
Organoids are starting to be deployed at multiple stages of this pipeline.
Toxicity screening.
This is the area where organoid use is most advanced.
Liver toxicity (hepatotoxicity) is one of the leading causes of drug failure — drugs that look fine in mice and rats turn out to damage human livers.
Liver organoids, particularly when derived from human cells, predict hepatotoxicity better than rodent models in many cases.
Major pharmaceutical companies now include human liver organoids in their hepatotoxicity testing panels.

Similarly, kidney organoids for nephrotoxicity, cardiac organoids for cardiotoxicity, gut organoids for GI toxicity, and brain organoids for neurotoxicity are all being deployed for early-stage screening to filter out compounds with safety problems before they progress to expensive later-stage testing.
Target identification and validation.

When researchers identify a new candidate drug target — a protein that, when inhibited, might treat a disease — they need to confirm that hitting that target in human cells actually produces the desired effect.
Organoids are increasingly used for this.
If you're working on a colorectal cancer drug, you can knock out your candidate target in colorectal organoids and see what happens.
If you're working on an Alzheimer's drug, you can knock out the target in cortical organoids and look for the effect on neurons.
This kind of human-cell target validation is much stronger evidence than equivalent work in mice.
Phenotypic screening.

This is the approach where you don't have a specific target in mind but instead screen compounds for their ability to produce a desired phenotype — say, restoring normal function to a diseased organoid.
Cystic fibrosis organoid screening, where compounds are tested for their ability to restore CFTR function (measured by the forskolin swelling assay we mentioned), is one example.
The Vertex drug ivacaftor was originally identified in a CFTR-functional screen, and organoid-based versions of similar screens are now common.
Disease modeling.
Many diseases lack good animal models, especially diseases with strong human-specific genetic components.
Organoids derived from patient iPSCs can recapitulate disease phenotypes in human cells, providing models that mouse studies can't.

Spinal muscular atrophy, Rett syndrome, Timothy syndrome, microcephaly, polycystic kidney disease, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency — all have published organoid models that have generated insight unavailable in other systems.
The practical consequence of all this is that pharmaceutical R&D is undergoing a slow but real shift toward including organoid-based assays at multiple stages.
Twenty years ago, the standard preclinical model for many compounds was a rodent.
Today, it's increasingly a rodent plus a panel of human organoid assays.
Ten years from now, my expectation is that organoid assays will be the primary preclinical evaluation for many indications, with rodent studies playing a more limited role.
The 2022 FDA Modernization Act 2.0 mentioned earlier removed a longstanding regulatory requirement that all new drug applications include animal testing data.
The new statute explicitly allows non-animal methods — including organoids, organ-on-chip systems, and computational models — to substitute for animal data where appropriate.
This regulatory shift is a major enabling event.
It means companies can invest in organoid-based development programs without worrying that the FDA will demand redundant animal work.
It will accelerate the migration of drug development toward human-cell-based models.
There are still real challenges.
Organoid assays need to be validated and standardized before they can fully replace established assays.
Different organoid protocols give different results.
Variability between organoid batches and between iPSC lines is a real issue.
The regulatory infrastructure for accepting organoid data is still maturing.
But the trajectory is clear.
Drug discovery is, gradually but unmistakably, becoming an organoid-and-chip enterprise, with the goal of more human-relevant predictions and lower attrition rates.
The economic stakes here are enormous.
A pharmaceutical company spends, on average, $2 to $3 billion to bring one drug through development.

If organoids can reduce attrition rates by even a small percentage, the savings are massive, and so is the public health benefit — more useful drugs reaching patients faster, with fewer late-stage failures eating up resources.
